record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (elected official; holds deeds, mortgages, liens, plats)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Clerk (wills, estate settlements, inventories; no separate probate court in KY)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage bonds from 1780s; licenses from 1852; state registration 1958)

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Kentucky Burned Counties; KDLA county records guides; Kentucky Encyclopedia (John E. Kleber, ed.); county histories at Kentucky Historical Society
administrative_notes: Kentucky was Virginia's Kentucky County (1776), divided into Fayette, Jefferson, and Lincoln counties (1780). Admitted as the 15th state on 1 June 1792 with nine counties (Bourbon, Fayette, Jefferson, Lincoln, Madison, Mason, Mercer, Nelson, Woodford). Pre-1792 records are at the Library of Virginia (Richmond) and KDLA (Frankfort). Kentucky was a slave state that did not secede — a critical border state in the Civil War with both Union and Confederate soldiers. The Kentucky County Clerk is an elected constitutional officer who holds deeds, marriages, wills, and other vital records. Virginia military bounty warrants distributed land south of the Green River. Daniel Boone's Wilderness Road (1775) was the primary settlement route through Cumberland Gap.

core_repositories.source_name: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives (KDLA)
core_repositories.description: Primary state repository. Houses microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, wills, tax lists, court orders), vital records, military records, state government records, and land grants. Microfilm loan program available through local libraries. Pre-1792 Virginia-era Kentucky records. Confederate pension files.

core_repositories.source_name: Kentucky Historical Society — Thomas D. Clark Center for Kentucky History
core_repositories.description: Major research library with manuscript collections, published county histories, family papers, photographs, maps, and the Kentucky Ancestor magazine (1965+). Martin F. Schmidt Research Library. Kentucky military records index. Essential complement to KDLA.

Outstanding manuscript collections for Kentucky and the Ohio Valley. Family papers, business records, maps, photographs, and published genealogies. Special Collections on the Filson Club History Quarterly (1926+). Particularly strong for Louisville/Jefferson County, Bluegrass, and early Kentucky settlement records.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Kentucky Special Collections Research Center
core_repositories.description: Major research center for Kentucky genealogy. Appalachian collections, oral history recordings, Kentucky coal industry records, photographs, county records on microfilm, and the Kentuckiana Digital Library. Louie B. Nunn Center for Oral History. King Library Press publications.

Pre-1792 Virginia-era records for Kentucky. Land patents, surveys, Virginia military bounty warrants, county court records for Fayette, Jefferson, and Lincoln counties when they were part of Virginia. Essential for any research predating Kentucky statehood.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court orders), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. Extensive microfilm partnership with KDLA provides digitized county records.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, burned county information, and links to digitized microfilm.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Kentucky newspapers. Includes Kentucky Gazette (Lexington, 1787 — one of the earliest newspapers west of the Alleghenies), Louisville papers, and county weeklies.
Digitized Kentucky newspapers beyond Chronicling America. County weeklies document legal notices, estate settlements, land sales, marriage announcements, and community events across rural Kentucky.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Tax lists naming slaveholders and number enslaved (1780s-1865), estate inventories naming enslaved individuals, manumission records, bills of sale, and county court order books with slave patrol records. Kentucky was a slave state that did not secede — unique border state context.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for Kentucky (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, and complaint files. Kentucky Freedmen's Bureau records are extensive because emancipation came with the 13th Amendment (Dec 1865), not the Emancipation Proclamation (which exempted loyal states).

Camp Nelson (Jessamine County) was a major Union supply depot and African American recruitment center. Over 10,000 United States Colored Troops (USCT) soldiers enlisted here. Records document soldiers, their families, and refugee camps. Camp Nelson National Monument (2018).
Born in Slavery: Slave Narratives from the Federal Writers' Project (1936-1938). Kentucky narratives provide first-person accounts of enslaved life in the Commonwealth.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: Kentucky was hunting ground for Cherokee, Shawnee, and other nations — not permanently settled. Records include: Treaty of Sycamore Shoals (1775), Fort Stanwix (1768), and Cherokee removal records (Trail of Tears passed through western Kentucky, 1838-39). Chickasaw cession (Jackson Purchase, 1818) opened far western Kentucky.

military_records.description: Confederate pension applications, Adjutant General records, militia records, Revolutionary War warrants, War of 1812 records, and WWI/WWII records. Kentucky was a border state — both Union and Confederate service records exist. Confederate pensions filed at KDLA; Union pensions at NARA.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers & Sailors System — Kentucky
military_records.description: Database of Union and Confederate soldiers from Kentucky. Approximately 100,000 Kentuckians served in Union forces and 25,000-40,000 in Confederate forces. Cross-reference with KDLA and NARA pension records.

land_and_property.repository: Library of Virginia, Richmond / KDLA, Frankfort
land_and_property.description: Pre-statehood land records: Virginia land patents, military bounty warrants (especially south of Green River), surveys, and treasury warrants for Kentucky District. Published in Willard Rouse Jillson's 'The Kentucky Land Grants' and 'Old Kentucky Entries and Deeds.' Virginia's system did not use a federal grid — metes and bounds descriptions.

land_and_property.source_name: Kentucky Land Office — State Land Grants (1792+)
land_and_property.description: Post-statehood land grants issued by the Commonwealth of Kentucky. Grant books, surveys, and patents. South of Green River land was distributed via Virginia military warrants; north was via treasury warrants. Jillson's compilations provide published indexes.

vital_records.source_name: Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services — Vital Statistics
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1911 (compliance improved ~1917). For earlier events, check county records: marriages from county formation, deaths from church and cemetery records, births from family Bibles and county records. Death certificates (1911+) and birth certificates (1911+) at KDLA after 75/100 year transfer.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Kentucky Church Records at KDLA and KHS
church_and_cemetery.description: Baptist (strongest denomination in KY), Methodist, Christian Church (Disciples of Christ — Stone-Campbell movement originated in KY), Presbyterian, and Catholic records. Cane Ridge Revival (1801) records. Shaker records (Pleasant Hill, South Union). Catholic records extensive in Nelson, Marion, and Washington counties (Holy Cross, Loretto, Bardstown).

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Kentucky. Coverage varies across 120 counties. Family cemeteries on farms and in mountain hollows are important supplements to official records. Kentucky Genealogical Society cemetery surveys also valuable.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes Bluegrass Roots (quarterly) and maintains research resources. Annual conference and workshops.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, historical sketches, and county-specific research guides for all 120 Kentucky counties.

Authoritative reference for Kentucky history based on 'The Kentucky Encyclopedia' (John E. Kleber, ed.). County histories, biographical entries, and cultural topics. Useful for understanding county formation and local history.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Kentucky has no abolished counties, but Kentucky County, Virginia (1776-1780) is the predecessor jurisdiction for all of Kentucky. It was divided into Fayette, Jefferson, and Lincoln counties in 1780, which were still Virginia counties until statehood in 1792. Researchers tracing pre-1780 ancestors should search Kentucky County, VA records at the Library of Virginia. Additionally, the original nine counties at statehood (1792) — Bourbon, Fayette, Jefferson, Lincoln, Madison, Mason, Mercer, Nelson, and Woodford — were parent counties for all subsequent Kentucky counties. James City (proposed in 1792, never organized) is sometimes referenced but was never a functioning county.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Kentucky experienced significant courthouse record losses, particularly during the Civil War. As a border state with active guerrilla warfare, several courthouses were deliberately burned. Notable losses: Adair (1864 guerrilla raid), Breathitt (1873 fire during feuds era), Clay (1862 Civil War), Clinton (1864 Civil War), Cumberland (1864 Civil War), Knox (1863 Civil War). Additional losses from accidental fires: Bath (1844), Calloway (1842), Estill (1860), Gallatin (1838), Morgan (1939), Owsley (1929). KDLA microfilm and FamilySearch digitization help compensate for losses.
